note: Default polite greeting. Use with strangers, shopkeepers, officials. Informal version: just 'Bună.'
context: You walk into a pharmacy. The woman behind the counter looks up at you.

note: Friends and peers only. Don't say this at customs.
context: You spot your tutor's friend at the café. You've met once before.
You've paid at the market stall. The seller wraps up your vegetables and smiles.

note: Add 'mult' for 'thanks a lot': 'Mulțumesc mult.'
context: The waiter brings your coffee. You look up and say:

note: Polite form. Informal is 'te rog'. Literally 'I ask you'.
context: You point at the item behind the glass and slide your money across the counter.
Someone thanks you for holding the lift door. You smile and say:

note: Use to get attention or apologize. Informal: 'scuză-mă'.
context: Crowded trolleybus. You need to squeeze past someone to reach the door.

note: Lit. 'it seems bad to me'. Use for genuine apology, not just bumping someone.
context: You accidentally knock someone's coffee off the table in a café. They look up.

note: Slavic loan — same word as Russian 'да'.
context: The customs officer asks: 'Is this your first visit to Moldova?'
A street vendor holds out a souvenir and asks if you want to buy it.

note: Formal 'you'. 'Vorbiți' is the polite form of 'speak'.
context: The pharmacist starts explaining your medication in rapid Romanian. You're lost.

note: Useful in Moldova — Russian is widely understood.
context: You're lost and stop someone on the street. Romanian isn't working. Try Russian.
Someone speaks to you at length, clearly expecting a response. Be honest.
The shopkeeper asks if you speak Romanian. You don't want to say no completely.
A taxi driver explains something, gesturing at the road. You have absolutely no idea.
The woman at the counter said the price but it was too fast. You need to hear it again.
Your host is giving you directions to the market. Way too fast. You need to slow them down.
Border control. The officer looks at your passport and asks why you're visiting.

note: Swap 'o săptămână' for 'două săptămâni' (two weeks) or 'zece zile' (ten days).
context: The officer is still looking at you. Now she asks how long you're staying.

note: Definite article in Romanian is a SUFFIX: 'pașaport' → 'pașaportul' (the passport).
context: The guard at the desk holds out his hand without looking up.
You approach the green channel at customs. An officer steps forward.
Last question before the stamp: where are you staying?
Beautiful hand-painted pot at the market. No price tag anywhere.

note: 'Aș dori' is the polite conditional 'I would like' — softer than 'vreau' (I want).
context: You're at the bakery. You point at the pastry behind the glass.

note: Swap 'pâine' for any item: apă (water), lapte (milk), ouă (eggs).
context: Small corner shop. Shelves are behind the counter. You need to ask.

note: Note the suffix: 'toaletă' (a toilet) → 'toaleta' (the toilet).
context: You've been walking Chișinău all day. You step into a café and urgently need a bathroom.

note: 'Apă plată' = still water; 'apă minerală' = sparkling. Important — defaults vary.
context: You sit down at a restaurant table. The waiter approaches with a notepad.

note: 'O' is the indefinite article for feminine nouns; 'un' for masculine. 'Cafea' is feminine.
context: Morning. Small café near your hotel. One other customer. The barista looks at you.
At the checkout. The cashier says the total. You reach for your phone.

note: Moldova: cash still common at markets, ATMs widely available in cities.
context: Market stall. Seller looks doubtful when she sees your phone. You pull out cash instead.
Transaction done. The cashier is already looking at the next customer. You need the receipt.

note: Useful at markets where bargaining is OK; not at fixed-price shops.

ro: O masă pentru doi, vă rog.

context: You sit down at a restaurant table. The waiter approaches and asks what you'd like.
The menu is all in Romanian. You decide to just trust the waiter.
The waiter passes by and nods at your plate, asking silently if everything is ok.
You've finished. You catch the waiter's eye across the room.
You slip on wet cobblestones near the market. People are walking past.
The heat and unfamiliar food have caught up with you. You need to sit and explain.

note: Worth recognizing — someone may say this to you.
context: An elderly woman is struggling with heavy bags at the bus stop. You approach.
You have a headache and no medicine. You stop someone on the street.
Your bag has been snatched. A bystander is frozen. You need them to act.

note: This is the counting form. With a noun, use 'un' (m) or 'o' (f): 'un leu', 'o cafea'.
'Doi' is masculine. Feminine is 'două' — 'două cafele' (two coffees), 'două ape' (two waters). Gender of the noun determines which to use.
Same word as 'new' (feminine) — context decides which one is meant.
Often shortened to 'unșpe' in fast speech. Same goes for 12-19 (doișpe, treișpe, etc.).
Spoken: often 'doișpe'. Time: 'ora doisprezece' = twelve o'clock.
Literally 'two tens'. From 20 upward, you need 'de' before a noun: 'douăzeci de lei' (20 lei).
Two hundred = 'două sute'. Five hundred = 'cinci sute'.
In Moldova, prices over 1000 lei are common for hotels/groceries-for-the-week. Two thousand = 'două mii'.
Numbers 1-19 connect directly to the noun: 'zece lei', 'cinci lei'. No 'de' needed.
Important rule: from 20 upward, insert 'de' before the noun. 'Cincizeci de lei' (50), 'o sută de lei' (100). Without 'de' it sounds wrong.
Feminine 'două' because 'cafea' is feminine (plural 'cafele'). For masculine plurals use 'doi': 'doi pacienți' (two patients).
When paying cash with a big bill. 'Rest' = change owed back to you.
Used until about midday. 'Dimineață' = morning.
Used after ~6pm. 'Seară' = evening.
Literal: 'it seems good to me'. Said when meeting someone for the first time.
'Încântată' is feminine. Men say 'Încântat'. You'd use 'Încântată' since you're a woman.
Informal 'you'. Formal: 'Cum vă numiți?' Worth recognizing even if you won't ask it.
Literally 'I call myself Diana'. More formal than 'Sunt Diana' (I am Diana).

en: How are you? (casual, lit. 'what more are you doing?')
note: Very common. Informal. The 'mai' softens it — not 'what are you doing' but a friendly check-in.
Standard response to 'Ce mai faci?' Switch 'tu' to 'dumneavoastră' for formal: 'Bine, și dumneavoastră?'

en: Thank you very much! (lit. 'thank you beautifully')
Here you are! / Please, go ahead! / Come in!
note: Very versatile. Said when handing something over, inviting someone in, or telling someone to go ahead. You'll hear this a lot.
Very common farewell in shops and offices. The polite reply is 'Mulțumesc, și dumneavoastră!'
Used as a toast when drinking AND as a wish of good luck. Very Moldovan.
Your trip month! 'Pe primul iulie' = on July 1st.
Swap the day name as needed. 'Azi' = today, 'mâine' = tomorrow.
Dates: 'pe primul' = on the first. 'Pe doi iulie' = on July 2nd.

context: You walk up to the bank counter with dollars in hand.

note: 'Curs de schimb' = exchange rate. Ask this before exchanging — rates vary a lot between banks and exchange offices.
context: Before handing over your cash, you need to know today's rate.

en: I want to open an account.
context: You'll be staying a while. You sit down at the bank officer's desk.

note: 'A retrage' = to withdraw. ATM = 'bancomat'.
context: The ATM ate your card. You go inside to the teller window.

ru: Я хочу положить деньги на счёт.
context: You just got paid in cash and want to put it in your account.

note: 'Card' is used — same word as in English/Russian.
context: Small restaurant. No card reader visible anywhere. Better to ask before ordering.

ro: Unde este bancomatul cel mai apropiat?
note: 'Bancomat' = ATM. 'Cel mai apropiat' = nearest.
context: Your cash ran out at the market. You stop a passerby.
The teller is about to process your exchange. You want to know the full cost first.

en: I want to make a transfer.
context: You need to send money home. You approach the bank window.

ro: Câți lei primiți pe un dolar?
en: How many lei do you give per dollar?
note: Direct way to ask the rate when exchanging USD. Swap 'dolar' for 'euro' as needed.
context: Exchange office near the central market. You have $200 to change.

en: I want to renew my ID card.
note: 'Buletin' = Moldovan national ID card. 'A reînnoi' = to renew.
context: You're at the desk at the civil registry office. The clerk looks up at you.

note: 'Acte' = documents/papers. Very useful at any government office.
context: You called ahead but didn't understand everything. Now you're at the window.

note: 'Ghișeu' = service window at a bank, post office, or government office.
context: Large government building. Many rooms. You're not sure which window to queue at.

en: How many days does it take?
note: 'A dura' = to take (time). Can also say 'Cât durează?' = How long does it take?
context: You submitted your documents. Now you need to know when to come back.

en: Do I need to fill out a form?
note: 'Formular' = form. 'A completa' = to fill in/out.
context: The clerk is explaining the process. You want to make sure you understand the steps.

ro: Am o programare la ora unsprezece.
en: I have an appointment at eleven o'clock.
note: Swap the time as needed. 'Programare' = appointment/booking.
context: You arrive at the office. The receptionist asks if you have an appointment.

en: Can I make an appointment online?
context: You don't want to queue. You ask the clerk if there's a faster way.

note: 'A expira' = to expire. Same for buletin: 'Buletinul meu a expirat.'
context: You're explaining to the officer why you need a new document.

note: 'Primărie' = city hall / municipal office. Many document services go through here.
context: You need to register something officially. You stop someone on the street to ask.

note: 'Iată' = here is / here are. Useful when handing papers to an official.
context: The clerk holds out her hand. You open your folder.

en: Thank you very much for your help.
note: 'Frumos' (beautiful) added after 'mulțumesc' makes it warmer — like 'thank you kindly'.
context: A stranger spent 10 minutes helping you find the right bus. You're genuinely grateful.

ro: Nu am înțeles. Puteți repeta, vă rog?
en: I didn't understand. Can you repeat, please?
note: Combines 'nu înțeleg' (present) with perfect tense — 'nu am înțeles' = I didn't understand (just now).
context: The official just explained something important. You caught almost none of it.

ro: Pot să vă pun o întrebare?
en: May I ask you a question?
note: Polite way to open a conversation with a stranger or official.
context: You need to interrupt someone who looks busy. This is the polite opener.

note: 'Îmi pare rău' = I'm sorry. 'A întârzia' = to be late.
context: You rush into your tutor's apartment 15 minutes late. She opens the door.

ro: Unde pot găsi un loc să mănânc?
en: Where can I find a place to eat?
ru: Где я могу найти место поесть?
note: 'A găsi' = to find. 'A mânca' = to eat. 'Loc' = place.
context: It's 1pm, you don't know the neighbourhood, and you're starving.

ro: Cât timp durează până la centru?
en: How long does it take to get to the center?
note: Building on time vocab. 'Centru' = city center. Swap for any destination.
context: You're at a bus stop outside the city. Trying to plan your day.

en: Do you speak Romanian or Russian?
note: Moldova is bilingual. Useful opener — many people are more comfortable in Russian.
context: You meet someone new and don't know which language to use.

note: Pattern: trei (3) + spre + zece (10) = 3 towards 10. All 11-19 follow this.
'Pai' is a shortening of 'patru' (4). You'll hear both 'paisprezece' and 'patrusprezece'.
'Șai' from 'șase' (6). Romanians love contracting the teens.
Memory hook: șapte rhymes with lapte (milk). Șaptesprezece = 17.
Also written 'nouă spre zece' — same thing. Your tutor wrote it as two words.
'Și' = and. All compound numbers from 21 onward use 'și': douăzeci și unu, douăzeci și doi...
Notice 'doi' (masculine two) — matches a masculine noun. For feminine/neuter: 'douăzeci și două'.
patru (4) + zeci (tens). No surprises here.
'Șai' again — same contraction as in 16. Romanian hundreds are cleaner than the tens.
Swap your name in. 'Eu sunt' = I am. Pronoun 'eu' often dropped in conversation — just 'Sunt Diana.' works too.
Word order is reversed vs English: 'numele meu' = 'name my'. Romanian puts the noun first, possessive second.

ro: Eu am treizeci și trei de ani.
note: Romanian says 'I have X years', not 'I am X years old'. Notice 'de' before 'ani' — required from 20 upward.
Literal: 'How many years do you have?' Much more logical than English once you get used to it.
'Să ai' = subjunctive wish form — 'may you have'. 'Zi bună' alone (informal): you're stating the day is good. With 'Să ai' — you're wishing it.
Shorter, casual. Difference from 'Bună ziua': 'Bună ziua' = greeting (hello). 'Zi bună' = parting wish (goodbye variant).

ro: Îmi pare bine să te cunosc!
note: Literal: 'To me it seems good to know you'. 'Îmi pare' = it seems to me. 'Să te cunosc' = to know you.
Drop 'eu' in speech — just say 'am'. Context always clear from ending.
'Tu' optional but common for clarity. 'Ai' alone = you have.
El = he, ea = she. Same verb form 'are' for both — Romanian simplifies here.
'Avem' — notice the stem shifts to 'av'. Keep 'noi' for clarity; dropping it can confuse listeners.
'Voi' = you all. 'Aveți' is also the formal singular — you'd use this with a stranger or official instead of 'tu ai'.
Ei = they (masculine/mixed group). Ele = they (all feminine). Same verb 'au' — nice and short.

ro: Am douăzeci și șapte de ani.
note: No 'eu' needed — 'am' already means 'I have'. This is the natural way a Romanian would say it.
Combines 'câți ani' (how many years) + 'are' (she has) + 'ea' (she). Try swapping: 'Câți ani are el?' = How old is he?
Puts 'avem' into context. Same pattern: 'Eu am un bilet' (I have a ticket), 'Tu ai timp?' (Do you have time?).
